          "& [data-avatar-badge='status']": sizeConfig.badge.status,
?

content.text:
Yes — those lines are MUI `sx` nested CSS selectors.

From `libs/react-components/src/lib/Avatar/AppAvatar.tsx`:

```tsx
<Box
  sx={{
    position: "absolute",
    bottom: -3,
    right: -3,
    "& [data-avatar-badge='avatar']": sizeConfig.badge.avatar,
    "& [data-avatar-badge='image']": sizeConfig.badge.image,
    "& [data-avatar-badge='status']": sizeConfig.badge.status,
  }}
>
  {badge}
</Box>
```

What they mean:

- `&` = “this Box”
- `[data-avatar-badge='avatar']` = “any descendant element with `data-avatar-badge="avatar"`”
- so:

```tsx
"& [data-avatar-badge='avatar']": sizeConfig.badge.avatar
```

means:

> Inside this positioned badge container, find an element marked as `data-avatar-badge="avatar"` and apply the styles from `sizeConfig.badge.avatar`.

Same idea for `"image"` and `"status"`.

Why this exists:
- `badge` is passed in as a generic `ReactNode`
- `AppAvatar` doesn’t know exactly what component you render there
- so it uses `data-avatar-badge` attributes as hooks to style the badge differently depending on its type

Example:

```tsx
<AppAvatar
  size={40}
  badge={<Box data-avatar-badge="status" sx={{ borderRadius: "50%", bgcolor: "green" }} />}
/>
```

For size `40`, this will apply:

```ts
status: { width: "12px", height: "12px" }
```

If instead you render:

```tsx
<Box data-avatar-badge="avatar" />
```

then it gets:

```ts
avatar: { width: "16px", height: "16px", borderRadius: "6px" }
```

So the three selectors are basically a type-based sizing system for whatever badge element you render.

Quick summary:
- `"avatar"` → badge is another tiny avatar
- `"image"` → badge is an image/icon-like square
- `"status"` → badge is a small status dot

If you want, I can also show you a concrete rendered example of what `badge` is expected to look like in this component.
